edges.description: Ingested organisms reach the intestinal lumen and begin colonization.
edges.source: Exposure to contaminated food or water

edges.description: Mucus traversal positions bacteria for epithelial attachment.
Secretion-associated virulence supports barrier-targeting invasion progression.
Junctional cleavage permits bacterial passage across the epithelial layer.
Barrier breach amplifies host inflammatory signaling and epithelial damage.
edges.target: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

edges.description: Epithelial damage and fluid/electrolyte loss drive diarrheal output.
edges.source: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

edges.description: Mucosal injury and inflammatory colitis contribute to blood in stool.
edges.source: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

edges.description: Inflammatory enteritis and tissue injury contribute to cramping pain.
edges.source: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

edges.description: Immune activation can persist beyond acute infection in a subset of patients.
edges.source: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

edges.description: Post-infectious autoimmunity can manifest as peripheral neuropathy.
Persistent gut dysfunction can follow infectious colitis in a subset of patients.

nodes.description: Patients commonly experience severe cramping abdominal pain during acute illness.

evidence.snippet: Campylobacter jejuni is likely the most common bacterial cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, responsible for millions of cases of inflammatory diarrhea characterized by severe abdominal cramps and blood in the stool.

nodes.description: Invasive inflammatory disease may manifest with blood in stool.

evidence.snippet: Campylobacter jejuni is likely the most common bacterial cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, responsible for millions of cases of inflammatory diarrhea characterized by severe abdominal cramps and blood in the stool.

nodes.description: Campylobacter adhesins bind fibronectin on polarized epithelial cells, triggering host signaling needed for invasion.

evidence.snippet: Fibronectin at the basolateral side of polarized epithelial cells serves as binding site for adhesins CadF and FlpA, leading to intracellular signaling, which again triggers membrane ruffling and reduced host cell migration by focal adhesion.

nodes.description: C. jejuni motility enables directional migration through mucus to reach epithelial targets.

evidence.snippet: Chemotactically controlled motility in viscous milieu allows targeted navigation to intestinal mucus and colonization.

nodes.description: Acute inflammatory diarrhea is the central clinical presentation of campylobacteriosis.

evidence.snippet: Campylobacter jejuni is likely the most common bacterial cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, responsible for millions of cases of inflammatory diarrhea characterized by severe abdominal cramps and blood in the stool.

nodes.description: Infection is initiated by ingestion of Campylobacter from contaminated foods, untreated water, or animal-associated sources.
nodes.id: Exposure to contaminated food or water

evidence.snippet: Campylobacter infection is primarily transmitted through the consumption of contaminated food, especially uncooked meat, or untreated water; contact with infected animals or contaminated environments; poultry is the primary reservoir and source of human transmission.

nodes.description: A rare post-infectious autoimmune neuropathy occurring after Campylobacter infection.

evidence.snippet: Late complications are reactive arthritis, Guillain–Barré syndrome, and Miller Fisher syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Triggers of Guillain-Barré Syndrome: Campylobacter jejuni Predominates.
evidence.snippet: C. jejuni is responsible for about a third of GBS cases.

nodes.description: Bacterial HtrA protease activity cleaves tight/adherens junction components, compromising epithelial integrity.

evidence.snippet: We recently discovered that C. jejuni serine protease HtrA disrupts intestinal epithelial barrier functions via cleavage of the tight and adherens junction components occludin, claudin-8 and E-cadherin.

nodes.description: Host inflammatory responses and epithelial lesions produce fluid/blood loss and symptomatic enteritis.
nodes.id: Mucosal inflammatory response and epithelial injury

evidence.snippet: Lesions of the epithelium result in loss of electrolytes, water, and blood, leading to diarrhea, which flushes out mucus containing C. jejuni.

nodes.description: Loss of epithelial junctional integrity allows Campylobacter to cross epithelial barriers and expand tissue-level injury.

evidence.snippet: Instead, we found that only bacteria with active protein biosynthesis effectively cleave junctional proteins, which is followed by paracellular transmigration of C. jejuni through the epithelial cell layer.

nodes.description: Some patients develop delayed extraintestinal immune-mediated complications after acute Campylobacter infection.

evidence.snippet: Late complications are reactive arthritis, Guillain–Barré syndrome, and Miller Fisher syndrome.

nodes.description: A subset of patients develop chronic bowel dysfunction after resolved Campylobacter enteritis.

evidence.snippet: Also patients with infectious colitis, in particular Salmonella and Campylobacter, are at increased risk of postinfectious irritable bowel syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease on long-term follow-up.

nodes.description: Campylobacter relies on type III and type VI secretion-associated programs to establish an intestinal niche and potentiate host-cell invasion.

evidence.snippet: We conclude that C. jejuni does not possess a type IV secretion system and relies on the type III and type VI secretion systems to establish a niche and potentiate disease.
